Anatomy · Head & Neck

Cervical Plexus

Superficial & deep branches · Root levels · Distribution & function

Overview Superficial Deep High-Yield Rapid Review
1

Big-Picture Organization

The cervical plexus is formed by the ventral rami of C1–C4. It divides cleanly into a superficial (cutaneous/sensory) arm and a deep (motor) arm.

Root origin
C1–C4
Ventral rami
Superficial branches
4 nerves
Cutaneous, mostly sensory
Key deep outputs
Ansa + Phrenic
Motor to infrahyoids & diaphragm
Emergence point
Erb's point
Posterior border of SCM
Framework: Superficial = skin distribution of the neck, ear & shoulder. Deep = motor targets (infrahyoids, deep neck muscles, diaphragm). Root levels are the glue for reconstructing the map.
2

Superficial Cervical Plexus

These branches are primarily sensory. All four emerge from deep fascia near the posterior border of the SCM at Erb's point and spread as a fan across the scalp, ear, neck, and upper chest.

NerveRootsSensory territory
Lesser occipitalC2Scalp behind the ear and lateral neck
Great auricularC2–C3Lower ear, skin over parotid gland, mastoid region. Anterior fibers → parotid; posterior fibers → back of auricle
Transverse cervicalC2–C3Anterior and lateral neck
SupraclavicularC3–C4Skin over the clavicle, shoulder, and upper chest (medial, intermediate, and lateral branches)
Erb's point: The clustered emergence of all four cutaneous branches at the midpoint of the posterior border of the SCM — a favourite exam orientation landmark and the site for regional nerve blocks.
Level shortcut: C2 = posterior scalp/behind ear · C2–C3 = ear + parotid + anterior neck · C3–C4 = clavicle + shoulder + upper chest.
3

Deep Cervical Plexus

The deep plexus is mainly motor. The three highest-yield items are the ansa cervicalis, the phrenic nerve, and the C1 hitchhiking rule.

C1–C3
Ansa cervicalis

Ansa Cervicalis

Motor loop for most infrahyoid (strap) muscles: sternohyoid, sternothyroid, and omohyoid. Superior root (C1–C2) descends with the hypoglossal nerve before separating. Inferior root (C2–C3) loops up to join it. Together they innervate the strap muscles involved in swallowing, hyoid positioning, and phonation.

C3–C5
Phrenic

Phrenic Nerve ⭐ High-yield

Major motor supply to the diaphragm. Arises primarily from C3–C5, running on the anterior surface of the anterior scalene. Clinically essential: cervical spinal injury above C3 = respiratory compromise. Remember: C3–C4–C5 keeps the diaphragm alive.

C1
via CN XII

C1 Hitchhiking Rule

C1 fibers briefly travel with the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII). They branch off to supply geniohyoid and thyrohyoid (not from ansa cervicalis). They also contribute the superior root of the ansa cervicalis.

C1–C4
Muscular

Segmental Muscular Branches

Supply deep cervical muscles including the scalenes, levator scapulae, longus capitis, longus colli, and rectus capitis anterior/lateralis. Communicating fibers with CN XI (C2–C4) carry proprioceptive input related to the SCM and trapezius.

Deep branchRootsMain role / target
Ansa cervicalisC1–C3Sternohyoid, sternothyroid, omohyoid
Phrenic nerveC3–C5Diaphragm
Segmental muscularC1–C4Scalenes, levator scapulae, prevertebral muscles
C1 via CN XIIC1Geniohyoid, thyrohyoid; superior root of ansa
Communicating with CN XIC2–C4Proprioception for SCM and trapezius
4

High-Yield Integration

C1 hitchhiking rule

C1 → CN XII → geniohyoid & thyrohyoid

C1 fibers piggyback on the hypoglossal nerve then peel off to supply geniohyoid and thyrohyoid, and contribute the superior root of ansa cervicalis. Neither geniohyoid nor thyrohyoid is innervated by CN XII itself.

Phrenic nerve rule

C3–C4–C5 keeps the diaphragm alive

Even if a slide emphasizes only C3 and C4, always remember the full clinical formula includes C5. Bilateral phrenic injury → complete diaphragm paralysis and respiratory failure.

Accessory nerve relationship

CN XI + cervical plexus (C2–C4)

Cervical plexus fibers communicate with CN XI for proprioceptive and sensory information from SCM and trapezius. CN XI provides the primary motor supply; cervical contributions are largely sensory/proprioceptive.

Ansa targets

Most infrahyoids — but not all

Ansa cervicalis innervates sternohyoid, sternothyroid, and omohyoid. Thyrohyoid (C1 via CN XII) and geniohyoid (C1 via CN XII) are the exceptions.

5

Rapid Review

Click to reveal the answer.

1
What are the roots of the cervical plexus?▾
Ventral rami of C1–C4.
2
Name the four superficial (cutaneous) branches.▾
Lesser occipital (C2) · Great auricular (C2–C3) · Transverse cervical (C2–C3) · Supraclavicular (C3–C4).
3
What is Erb's point?▾
The point at the midpoint of the posterior border of the SCM where all four cutaneous branches emerge from deep fascia — used as a nerve block site and orientation landmark.
4
Which nerve receives C1 fibers as hitchhikers, and where do those fibers go?▾
CN XII (hypoglossal). C1 fibers branch off to supply geniohyoid and thyrohyoid, and form the superior root of the ansa cervicalis.
5
Which muscles does the ansa cervicalis innervate?▾
Sternohyoid, sternothyroid, and omohyoid (infrahyoid strap muscles). Not thyrohyoid or geniohyoid.
6
What are the roots of the phrenic nerve?▾
C3–C4–C5. Remember: 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive.'
7
Which cervical plexus fibers communicate with CN XI, and what do they carry?▾
C2–C4 fibers communicate with CN XI, carrying proprioceptive/sensory information related to SCM and trapezius.
8
What skin territory does the great auricular nerve cover?▾
Lower ear, skin over the parotid gland, and the mastoid region.
9
What deep muscles do the segmental cervical branches supply?▾
Scalenes, levator scapulae, longus capitis, longus colli, and rectus capitis anterior/lateralis.
10
One-line summary of superficial vs deep plexus?▾
Superficial = skin map of neck and shoulder (sensory). Deep = motor to infrahyoids, deep neck muscles, and the diaphragm.
6

Exam-Style MCQs

Try the stem first, then click to reveal the best answer and the reasoning.

1
A patient loses cutaneous sensation over the lower auricle and the skin over the parotid gland after a superficial cervical plexus block at Erb's point. Which nerve was most likely anesthetized?
A. Lesser occipital nerve
B. Great auricular nerve
C. Transverse cervical nerve
D. Supraclavicular nerves
▾
Answer: B. Great auricular nerve. The great auricular nerve (C2-C3) supplies the lower auricle and skin over the parotid and mastoid region. The lesser occipital nerve serves scalp posterior to the auricle, the transverse cervical nerve serves the anterior neck, and the supraclavicular nerves descend to skin over the clavicle and shoulder.
2
A lesion interrupts C1 fibers after they travel with the hypoglossal nerve but before they branch to a muscle that elevates the larynx during swallowing. Which muscle would be denervated?
A. Sternohyoid
B. Sternothyroid
C. Omohyoid
D. Thyrohyoid
▾
Answer: D. Thyrohyoid. Thyrohyoid receives C1 fibers that hitchhike with CN XII rather than traveling through the ansa cervicalis. Sternohyoid, sternothyroid, and omohyoid are classic ansa cervicalis targets.
✕
